sources.notes: Statement and constraints: move on [0,s] with current step length, turning decreases power, and maximize final power.
Official editorial solution uses an O(1) large-s shortcut for s > k^2 and BFS over positions for the remaining small cases.
Luogu solution derives the k-2 large-case construction using residues modulo k-2 after moving with step k and then k-1.
Cnblogs writeup explains the state graph view (position, power, direction), the k^2 cutoff, and why the bounded BFS is fast enough.

annotation.summary: Starting from 0 with step length k, move within [0,s], optionally turning and losing one power each turn, and maximize the power when reaching s.
annotation.constraints: s <= 1e9, k <= 1000, and sum of k over test cases <= 2000. The solution cannot BFS over all positions unless s is reduced by a threshold argument.
annotation.core_idea: If s is divisible by k, go straight and keep power k. Otherwise, for sufficiently large s, one can always keep at least k-2 power: move right by k until near the end, turn left using k-1 steps enough times to adjust the residue modulo k-2, then turn right and finish using step k-2. Because each backward k-1 step changes the remaining distance modulo k-2 by 1, at most k-2 such steps are needed, and s > k^2 leaves enough room. Thus large cases answer max(1,k-2). For the remaining s <= k^2, build the reachable-state process explicitly. At a fixed power and direction, repeatedly moving without turning closes all positions along that arithmetic progression; after one turn, power decreases and direction flips. Processing these layers is a BFS over the number of turns, and the first layer that reaches s determines the maximum remaining power.
annotation.complexity: O(1) for s > k^2; O(s*k) worst-case for bounded BFS when s <= k^2, with k <= 1000
tricks: Use a large-case residue construction to avoid exploring positions up to 1e9.
Model turns as BFS layers because every turn decreases power by exactly one.
For a fixed direction and power, close all positions reachable by repeated strokes before considering the next turn.

solution_variants.name: large-case-residue-plus-bfs
solution_variants.summary: Return k if s is divisible by k; return max(1,k-2) if s > k^2; otherwise BFS reachable positions by decreasing power and alternating direction.
solution_variants.complexity: O(1) large case, O(s*k) small case

tags.evidence: For small s, the solution processes reachable positions layer by layer by number of turns, which is a BFS on the implicit state graph.
Maximizing final power is equivalent to minimizing the number of turns needed to reach s in the movement state graph.
The large-case construction adjusts the remaining distance modulo k-2 using backward moves of length k-1.
The solution splits cases at s > k^2: large s is solved by a formula/construction, while only small s is explored by BFS.
The proof for large s explicitly constructs a route with final power k-2.
The bounded small case explicitly explores reachable positions in the implicit graph.
